title: Regulating inattention in fee-based financial advice
abstract: We study the impact of disclosure and inattention on the decision to retain fee-based financial advice using a two-tiered natural regulatory experiment. Increased salience in fee disclosure raises the drop rate for advice, implying improved attention ‚ÄĒ particularly for relatively sophisticated investors. However, a novel auto-drop requirement for inattentive investors generates far more drops, implying limited attention despite salient disclosure ‚ÄĒ particularly for the unsophisticated. Contrary to studies of commission-based advice, we find that investors benefit from fee-based advice. Benefits are higher for less sophisticated investors, who tend to be detrimentally auto-dropped. Drops triggered by salient disclosure tend to be beneficial.

author.Roger M. Edelen: Virginia Tech University, Pamplin College of Business, United States
author.Kingsley Y.L. Fong*: University of New South Wales, UNSW Business School, Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia
author.Jingyi Han: University of New South Wales, UNSW Business School, Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

article.1. Introduction: Attention to the cost of financial services is a key consideration in household finance, as pointed out in a commentary on their general magnitude (Bogle, 2008), and a presidential address on the specific case of delegated portfolio management (French, 2008). The case of personal financial advice is similarly relevant. Advice plays a central role in the investment decisions of tens of millions of households, at an estimated cost of around $100 billion per year globally, or about 0.7% of advised assets.1 Ceteris paribus, this cost translates into a 20% erosion in cumulative savings over a notional thirty-two year investment horizon‚ÄĒa figure similar to the estimated capitalized cost of active management in French (2008).2 In short, the cost of financial advice surely warrants investors‚Äô attention.Yet, households can be prone to inattention, inertia, and poor decisions (Madrian and Shea, 2001). These tendencies are likely to be strong in the case of financial advice, because assessing costs and benefits of advice is complex and opaque (perhaps intentionally so, as in Carlin, 2009). Thus, the extent to which investors actively assess the advice decision is an important empirical question, as is the derivative question of whether regulators can improve deficiencies in investors‚Äô attention. Our study provides novel insights into these matters.Our analysis centers on a natural regulatory experiment uniquely suited to the topic ‚ÄĒ the 2013 adoption of the Australian law known as the Future of Financial Advice (FOFA). This law instituted three new requirements. First, mutual funds are prohibited from paying advisors who place investors into their funds, an arrangement referred to as conflicted remuneration by FOFA and revenue sharing in the U.S. Second, advisors must provide a Fee Disclosure Statement (FDS) to investors on each anniversary of advice. Third, advisers must provide an opt-in notice alongside the FDS every second anniversary. The opt-in notice warns that if the investor does not explicitly renew the advice service within 60 days (i.e., ‚Äėopt-in‚Äô), the service is automatically terminated the following month, which we call auto-drop.3We analyze the portfolio holdings of 265,000 clients of a large Australian financial planning firm who choose between fee-based advice or self-direction. Our data spans a ten-year window around FOFA inception, but it does not facilitate a comparative analysis of the ban on commission-based pay since the firm‚Äôs advice fee is directly charged to investors. However, our analysis does provide novel evidence of positive benefits from fee-based advice, contrary to much of the literature on commission-based advice (e.g., Bergstresser et al., 2008, Christoffersen et al., 2013, Del Guercio and Reuter, 2014). Our focus is on the two elements of FOFA that directly relate to attention ‚ÄĒ the FDS and auto-drop. In combination, these features are particularly well suited to analyzing whether investors periodically assess the costs and benefits of continuing advice (i.e., ‚Äėthe advice decision‚Äô). The FDS is a simple and direct ‚Äėplain English‚Äô document that states the dollars paid for advice; specific services provided; and nothing else (see Appendix A for an industry association template). This makes it a sharp instrument for assessing salience in fee disclosure. Auto-drop is a remarkably proactive mechanism that terminates an ongoing contract unless the investor explicitly approves the fee disclosure. These two features act in tandem to provide a direct measure of inattention to salient fee disclosure. To our knowledge, this two-tiered natural experiment in regulation is unique.We begin with an analysis of the diligence with which investors attend to the advice decision, and the impact of the FDS and opt-in nudges on that decision (see Thaler and Sunstein, 2009 for a general treatise on nudges). We find that investors maintain a modest degree of diligence in attending to the advice decision. The baseline (pre FDS) advice drop rate is about 0.75% per month. The FDS triggers an increase in the drop rate, cumulating to a statistically significant 4%‚Äď5% abnormal drops relative to baseline after six months. From this, we conclude that salient fee disclosure improves attention to the advice decision, relative to the opaque disclosure of the prior regulatory regime.The opt-in requirement (which we often abbreviate as opt-in) has a much more significant 13%‚Äď16% marginal impact on the cumulative advice drop rate (beyond baseline and FDS). This effect is discrete and largely aligned with the 60-day deadline for opting in, consistent with auto-drop. Moreover, the impact is permanent: only one percent of auto-drops subsequently reverse back into advice.Perhaps more importantly, because the opt-in notice arrives concurrent with an FDS, the 13%‚Äď16% rate of auto-drop provides a novel and direct measure of inattention to salient disclosure.4 This makes our setting unique: typical studies of disclosure can only measure affirmative responses. We conclude that salience in disclosure helps nudge investors to attend to their advice decision, but the effectiveness of salience is limited.We also consider the differential impact of FOFA nudges based on investor characteristics relating to investment sophistication, inertia, overconfidence, and other traits thought to affect investment decisions.5 We find clear evidence that the FDS has more impact for relatively sophisticated investors (e.g. wealthier, older, and retirement-focused investors) who are likely predisposed to attention. Conversely, relatively unsophisticated investors (young, less wealthy) are more frequently auto-dropped, consistent with inattention.An important question remains: Does the impact of FOFA nudges on investors‚Äô advice decision actually help investors? The notable disparity in impact for auto-drop vs. FDS leads to the disconcerting conclusion that FOFA may push investors out of potentially valuable financial advice, rather than induce voluntarily opt-out via salient fee disclosure. Two follow-on questions can help assess whether this amounts to over-nudging by FOFA. First, are those who value advice the ones most likely pushed out? And second, do the benefits that auto-dropped investors gained under advice persist after auto-drop?If one presumes that advice is harmful, as might be inferred from the evidence on commission-based advice, then any nudge out of advice is presumably helpful. But there seems little empirical basis for a presumption of harm in the case of fee-based advice, where conflict of interest is less obvious. Thus, we assess the benefits of fee-based advice both generally, and in relation to specific investor characteristics. We then investigate whether FOFA differentially nudges out investors who receive fewer benefits.We find that compared to self-directed portfolios, advised portfolios have higher returns, lower fees, better diversification, and better alignment of equity exposure with age; implying investors benefit from fee-based advice. We then relate these benefits to the same investor characteristics used to assess differential impact of FOFA nudges on investors‚Äô advice decision. Even after accounting for service and advisory fees, our analysis reveals that fee-based advice significantly enhances net-of-service-fee returns for younger investors, and those with substantial assets, extensive investment histories, and employment.Given these critical differences among investors, we create an instrument for advice value using these investor characteristics. We then interact that instrument with each of the two FOFA nudges. We do this to assess whether each nudge generates a higher advice drop rate for investors who receive less benefit from advice. The FDS successfully discriminates investors ‚ÄĒ those receiving less benefit are more likely to drop following an FDS. This holds using both predicted benefits based on investor characteristics (i.e., ex ante) and realized benefits (i.e., ex post). However, auto-drop fails the discrimination test ‚ÄĒ we observe higher auto-drop among investors with higher predicted benefit. Also, there is no statistically reliable distinction using realized benefits.We conclude that the FDS ‚Äď which requires attention ‚Äď tends to steer relatively sophisticated investors towards the right decision on advice. Auto-drop ‚Äď which acts on inattention ‚Äď tends to steer relatively unsophisticated investors towards what appears to be the wrong decision on advice.Our conclusion on auto-drop comes with two caveats. First, advice benefits do not reliably reverse in the twelve months following auto-drop, suggesting investors are not worse off. Second, the service fee for advice is dollar-based in our dataset. This creates a bias against smaller investors (Thiel, 2022), who pay a relatively high percentage fee. Nevertheless, auto-dropped investors on average receive positive net-of-service-fee benefits.Our paper fills several gaps in the rapidly growing household finance and consumer protection literature. Previous work documents that funds distributed via advisors underperform directly sold funds, both before and after fees (Bergstresser et al., 2008, Christoffersen et al., 2013, Del Guercio and Reuter, 2014). Studies of advised investors‚Äô portfolios generally find better diversification but higher fees and lower returns. Some are based on risk-adjusted benchmarks (Foerster et al., 2017, Linnainmaa et al., 2021); others compare self-directed and advised investors (Kramer, 2012, Bhattacharya et al., 2012, Hackethal et al., 2012, Gaudecker, 2015, Calcagno and Monticone, 2015, Hoechle et al., 2017, Chalmers and Reuter, 2020). These studies do not consider fee-based advice. Our study expands this literature to the increasingly common regime of fee-based advice. Moreover, we provide the first direct estimates of how advice value relates to investor characteristics, which is critical to considering optimal choice and regulatory nudges.Theoretical models attribute the value of advice to informational and psychological sources (Inderst and Ottaviani, 2012b, Gennaioli et al., 2015). And they argue that a commission-based remuneration structure, coupled with an inability to evaluate advice quality, can lead to undesirable outcomes for advised investors (Inderst and Ottaviani, 2012a, Inderst and Ottaviani, 2012c, Stoughton et al., 2011). However, Chang and Szydlowski (2020) propose that banning conflicted fees may not improve investor welfare. Our evidence suggests ‚Äď but does not directly establish ‚Äď that fee-based advice could benefit consumers.Improving investor sophistication is a crucial dimension of investor protection (Stoughton et al., 2011). Our study supports this view. We provide large-sample evidence that echoes (Bhattacharya et al., 2012), who conclude that investors often lack awareness of a need for advice. Our findings illuminate the challenges faced by less sophisticated investors who benefit more from advice, but are less able to evaluate those benefits. Nevertheless, the efficacy of financial literacy programs is subject to debate. A major concern is their untargeted nature, and the long gap between education and decision-making (Lusardi and Mitchell, 2014, Fernandes et al., 2014, Kaiser et al., 2022).By contrast, decision-relevant nudges via disclosure (Hirshleifer and Teoh, 2003, Loewenstein et al., 2014); default actions such as automatic enrollment in retirement plans (Madrian and Shea, 2001); and low-cost default investment products (Chalmers and Reuter, 2020) offer more timely and direct solutions. Nudges have likewise been shown to be effective for increasing saving (Karlan et al., 2016) and avoiding bank fees (Stango and Zinman, 2014, Stango and Zinman, 2016). Yet, our evidence highlights how the optimal default action can be unclear, and that targeting investors via automatic nudges can be an errant regulatory strategy.The remainder of the paper is structured as follows. Section 2 describes the institutional context and the data. Section 3 presents our examination of the advice decision and evaluation of the impact of the FOFA nudges on investors‚Äô regular reevaluation of advice. Section 4 assesses the value of advice on portfolio quality metrics. Section 5 integrates these analyses to test whether the regulatory nudges improve advice decisions by directing those who benefit less from advice to drop the service. Section 6 concludes.
article.2. Institutional details and data: This section provides background on the Australian market and summarizes our data.2.1. The Australian asset management marketAs of December 2016 (the end of our sample), the Australian asset management market ranked sixth in the world with USD 2.1 trillion in assets under management, comparable to the U.S. per capita.6 The market is supported by a mandatory national retirement saving scheme known as superannuation, which had a compound annual growth rate of 9% in assets under management from 2000 to 2016, outperforming the U.S. growth rate of 5.5%. Relative to GDP, Australian pension assets ranked seventh in the world (1.31 √ó GDP) compared to the U.S. fifth rank (1.69 √ó GDP).7The Superannuation Guarantee (Administration) Act of 1992 mandates that employers contribute 9‚Äď9.5% (revised over time) of salary into a superannuation account for each employee during the sample period. The self-employed can also contribute, and employees have the option to make additional contributions. Retirement savings are primarily defined contributions. Superannuation income is taxed at 15%, compared to the personal tax rate on investments up to 45%. Like 401k plans in the U.S., tax benefits are accompanied by withdrawal restrictions based on age (60) and retirement.2.2. Data sourceWe use anonymous investor-level data from a large Australian financial planning firm on a confidential basis. The firm offers all investors access to a mutual-fund investment platform for a fee. Investors can choose (and switch anytime) between self-directed portfolio construction and maintenance, or retaining a financial advisor for an additional monthly fee. We observe the total service fees (charged as a dollar amount) deducted from investor accounts. This averages 0.56% of assets under advice per year for self-directed investors and 0.82% for advised investors. Effectively, the incremental fee for advice is 0.26% per year. Advisors do not receive commissions; the advice fee is independent of asset allocation, turnover, or fund selection.All investors receive rudimentary financial advice at account initiation8 and have access to the same investment platform, which includes approximately 900 mutual funds from providers considered reliable by the firm‚Äôs research team. Investors paying for advice also receive (1) a comprehensive formal annual review of their risk profile, portfolio performance, and financial plan, (2) quarterly (at least) telephone contact to address these issues, and (3) ongoing availability for ad hoc counsel and advice.The dataset includes over 14 million monthly records of 265,593 investors‚Äô mutual-fund holdings from 2006 (the first year of advice service) through 2016. Investor characteristics include birth year, gender, employment status, and account anniversary dates (the FDS date and opt-in notice date). Survivorship bias is not a concern, as investors who left the platform remain in the dataset. We apply bounds on specific variables to exclude apparent errors (e.g., age must be between 18 and 115).We match investor holdings to the Morningstar Direct database to gather fund returns, expense ratios, asset allocations, and other fund characteristics. The fund code identifies retirement (tax-advantaged superannuation and pension) and non-retirement (fully taxable) investment funds. While our primary analysis pools fund types, we also condition on fund type in the regression analyses.Table 1 provides a summary of the data. From Panel A, the number of investors increased from approximately 57,000 in 2006 to about 150,000 by the 2013 FOFA initiation date (we tabulate biennially for brevity). The fraction of advised investors in our dataset increased dramatically (as might be expected from a new service), reaching 20% of all investors by the end of the sample. The growth in superannuation contributed to the rapid expansion of the financial planning industry in Australia.9 The increase occurred primarily before the implementation of FOFA, after which it plateaued and experienced a slight decline (likely due to compliance and advisor-education costs, according to the firm). Investors who held at least one retirement fund constitute 79% of the sample.Table 1. Retirement refers to accounts that hold retirement-class mutual funds.2.3. Investor characteristicsThe literature suggests that, in part, investor characteristics determine the use of financial advisors. Lack of financial knowledge (Lusardi and Mitchell, 2014) or financial confidence (Gennaioli et al., 2015) are two reasons often cited. Chalmers and Reuter (2020) study a population of university pension plan members and find that the demand for advice from a broker decreases with investor knowledge (income, age, education) and confidence (being male). Similarly, Fong et al. (2020) use Finnish population share registry data to document that higher-income (sophisticated) investors are more likely to use discount brokers and that older men (particularly those over 45 years old) prefer full-service brokers. However, the evidence in broader contexts is mixed. Bhattacharya et al. (2012) and Calcagno and Monticone (2015) examine data from European banks and find that financially illiterate investors either decline free financial advice or disregard advice generally viewed as beneficial.Panel B of Table 1 summarizes investor characteristics in our sample. All variable definitions are provided in Appendix B. In subsequent regression analysis, we standardize all investor characteristics to facilitate comparison of their significance.Portfolio size is a proxy for wealth and sophistication (Calvet et al., 2007, Calvet et al., 2009). The mean portfolio size for advised investors is about $300,000, more than three times the mean for self-directed investors.10 This finding suggests that investor sophistication positively predicts the purchase of advice. Generally, the portfolio size of investors with at least one retirement fund is smaller than those without a retirement fund ($77.3k vs. $125.5k).The average investor age in our sample is 55 years, similar to Kramer (2012) and Calcagno and Monticone (2015), but slightly higher than Linnainmaa et al. (2021) (51.2) and Bhattacharya et al. (2012) (49‚Äď52.9). The average age in studies of company or university retirement plans is much younger, around 40 (Madrian and Shea, 2001, Chalmers and Reuter, 2020). In our sample, age is similar for investors with or without retirement funds.We expect investors with a longer investment history to have higher inertia, i.e., less likely to change advice status (Madrian and Shea, 2001). The average investor in our sample has 5.3 years of investment history ‚Äď 3.3 years for advised investors and 5.5 years for self-directed investors. This is similar to the 3.7 and 4.6 years for advised investors in Foerster et al. (2017) and Linnainmaa et al. (2021). We also use financial experience with an affiliated bank as a proxy for inertia. This averages 7.5 years and is 1.3 years longer for advised investors.Gender representation is nearly at parity, consistent with U.S. and Canadian studies (e.g., Chalmers and Reuter, 2020, Linnainmaa et al., 2021).11 About 73% of the investors are employed. We use employment status and male gender as proxies for confidence, which tends to reduce the use of financial advice. However, employment may also imply less time to manage investments, potentially increasing the demand for advice. Furthermore, the impact of labor income on optimal asset allocation is also influenced by correlation with stocks and bonds (Benzoni et al., 2007). Summary statistics relating to portfolio characteristics, such as returns, asset allocation, and diversification, are considered in Section 4.
article.3. The advice decision and impact of FOF: We begin Section 3 by relating the decision to initiate, add, or drop advice to investor characteristics to determine how investor sophistication, inertia, and overconfidence help shape demand for fee-based advice. We then study the impact of FOFA nudges on the advice decision using these baseline dependencies as controls.3.1. Cross-sectional model for the advice decisionWe assess the cross-sectional determinants of demand for advice using a probability model estimated over each of three decisions. The first is the initial advice decision that the literature focuses on (e.g., Bhattacharya et al., 2012, Chalmers and Reuter, 2020). In our case, that means the choice to be advised or self-directed at account inception. We also consider subsequent switches to or from advice, which the investor can do at any time. We use a linear probability model in all specifications to more easily assess the marginal effect and economic significance.12 As this is a cross-sectional regression, data is at the investor level with independent variables set to the last available values.Table 2 presents the estimates. From Column 1, there are 175,759 instances of new investors. Here, the dependent variable equals one for advised investors (18,594 instances) and zero for self-directed investors. When considering switches, the dependent variable is one for investors who make the indicated switch and zero for those who do not. Similarly, Column 2 shows 205,041 investors start as self-directed, with 16,467 later switching to advice. Additionally, Column 3 shows 21,473 investors start with advice, and 6500 later switch to self-directed (drop advice).The coefficient estimates from Table 2 indicate a strong and statistically reliable positive association between choosing advice and the variables log Assets and log Age. Conversely, inertia (log DaysInv and log DaysBk) identifies a reduced likelihood of a switch. Consistent with predictions from the over-confidence literature, the male indicator 1Male is negatively related to choosing advice. The indicators for employment 1Employed and holding retirement funds 1Retire positively correlates with choosing advice, but only the latter relates negatively to dropping advice. Overall, the data suggests that sophisticated investors tend to seek advice, while inertia tends to preserve existing behaviors, as supported by prior studies (see, e.g., Bhattacharya et al., 2012, Calcagno and Monticone, 2015, Madrian and Shea, 2001).Table 2. FOFA nudges and the advice decisionColumns three and five of Table 3 present the advice drop rates following FDS1 and FDS2/Opt-in. We construct (but do not tabulate) pseudo-events to benchmark these events. Pseudo-events include every pre-FOFA advice anniversary that has the corresponding duration in advice (i.e., 13 months for FDS1 and 25 months for FDS2/Opt-in). Columns labeled ‚ÄúAbnormal‚ÄĚ subtract these pseudo-event averages.FDS1 notices generate an abnormal drop rate of about 4% after six months, plateauing thereafter. This doubles the benchmark pre-FOFA drop rate, and that difference is statistically reliable.14 We conclude that improved salience in fee disclosure fosters greater attention to reassessing the advice decision. Over the first few months following FDS2/Opt-in notices, the abnormal advice drop rate is similar to (but moderately higher than) the case of FDS1. Most strikingly, the abnormal drop rate jumps to about 17% after three months. This abnormal drop rate in the third month is about seven times higher than the benchmark rate,15 indicating substantial cases of auto-drop (recall, auto-drop occurs within 30 days of failure to respond to the two-month deadline).The contrast between the drop rate of the FDS1 and the FDS2/Opt-in at three months suggests an inherent limit to the effectiveness of salient fee disclosure. Auto-drop derives from inaction in response to FDS2/Opt-in, which we consider inattention. However, it could be that inaction is voluntary, meaning the investor assessed FDS2 (and FDS1, for that matter) and decided to drop, but rather than act immediately they paid an additional three months (or fifteen months relative to FDS1) of fees and auto-dropped. Using the monthly dollar fees of auto-dropping investors, we compute the cost of a three-month deferral at $113 * 3 months = $340, which seems a high value of time to avoid a few clicks on a website. Nevertheless, it is plausible that some of the implied inattention from auto-drop is ‚Äėvoluntary‚Äô rather than inadvertent. Either way, we consider the observed frequency of auto-drop to be a direct measure of inattention to salient fee disclosure.3.4. Multivariate analysis of FOFA nudges‚Äô impact on the advice decisionWe perform a multivariate linear probability analysis for two reasons: to better understand the impact of the opt-in notice separate from the simultaneous FDS2; and to condition on investor characteristics. Advice drop rates conditional on investor characteristicsDetailed estimates of the bm‚čĮ coefficients from Eq. (1) are tabulated in the upper rows of Table 4. Conditional effects of investor characteristics are tabulated in the lower rows. We now focus on those conditional effects.Baseline events. The first column of Table 4 details heterogeneity in the cjBase coefficients across investor characteristics. The estimates confirm evidence in the literature (see Section 2.3) that less wealthy, male, and non-retirement investors are more likely to drop advice. Not surprisingly, higher inertia overall lowers the drop rate (all inferences reliable at 1%). Baseline drop rates do not vary with past abnormal returns, but they do inversely relate to factor returns (‚ąí0.003) suggesting that advisors share some credit/blame for the systematic component of returns. This result is both novel and important to the scope of financial advice, as it suggests that investor evaluation of the quality of advice focuses on asset allocation and risk management at a broader (factor) level. The results also parallel studies Berk and Van Binsbergen (2015) and Barber et al. (2016), who find that factor returns play an out-sized role in fund flows. Our results indicate that asset allocation surpasses fund selection as a consideration in evaluating advice.FDS events. The ‚ąí0.004 coefficient on log Assets interacted with the FDS indicator implies that the FDS accentuates the higher baseline drop rate for less wealthy investors. The FDS also triggers a higher drop rate for older investors and those with retirement funds. These results suggest a larger FDS impact for sophisticated investors. Conversely, inertia is associated with a smaller FDS impact. One of the strongest conditioning characteristics for FDS is past factor returns. The ‚ąí0.017 coefficient indicates that the FDS accentuates investors‚Äô assignment of some credit/blame for systematic returns on the adviser.Opt-in events. Proxies for low sophistication (young, less wealthy, no retirement fund) predict higher auto-drop frequency (all reliable at 1%). The inertia proxies do not provide clear inferences, but males have a higher auto-drop rate. Return results for auto-drop run somewhat counter to baseline and FDS events. Low abnormal returns predict a higher likelihood of auto-drop (coefficient ‚ąí0.058), but factor returns are not related to auto-drop frequency. To the extent auto-drop measures inattention, these results indicate that poor idiosyncratic returns reduce investor attention. This evidence is consistent with Sicherman et al. (2016) who likewise document reduced attention (online account log-ins) following poor returns.We conclude that disclosure has a relatively large impact on financially sophisticated investors, and that auto-drop has a relatively large impact on less sophisticated investors.
article.4. The value of fee-based advice: Effective regulation of financial advice should improve the alignment between the advice decision and the benefits from advice. However, this assumes inherent value in advice. This premise does not receive much support in the literature, where the focus is advice remunerated by commissions and other forms of revenue sharing. In Section 4, we assess the value of fee-based advice overall, and construct a model of advice value conditional on investor characteristics. We then apply estimates from that model to examine FOFA effectiveness in Section 5.4.1. Portfolio quality metricsWe assess the value of advice using two perspectives on portfolio quality: returns and portfolio characteristics. In assessing portfolio characteristics, such as equity allocation and diversification, we follow the literature (e.g., Bhattacharya et al., 2012, Kramer, 2012, Foerster et al., 2017, Li et al., 2014). However, most of our focus is on returns as that is the first-order consideration in investing.Financial advisors may affect fund returns through fund selectivity and/or asset allocation. Fund selectivity can add value once a basic allocation decision is set, and this (i.e., portfolio alpha) is the norm in performance assessment. However, the asset allocation decision is also potentially relevant‚ÄĒindeed, it may be the larger determinant of the overall investment outcome (Brinson et al., 1986, Brinson et al., 1991, Ibbotson and Kaplan, 2000, Calvet et al., 2007). Hence, our analysis considers both abnormal returns and the factor component of returns (see Appendix B for return factors considered).We compute value-weighted average abnormal returns using monthly fund holdings, both net and gross of fund fees.16 We also consider fund fees directly since they are an aspect of fund selection. Total service fees (denoted ServFees, which includes the advice fee for those advised) are excluded from both net and gross abnormal returns. By assessing abnormal returns net of fund fees, we evaluate the gross benefits of advice. Our study also examines returns after service fees to determine if the varied benefits of advice surpass the associated costs. Table 5 summarizes our measures. Consistent with much of the literature on mutual fund performance, the average net and gross monthly abnormal return is negative.17 Fund fees average 1.42% per year. As a first indication of value from advice, returns are higher (more precisely, less negative) for advised investors. This is primarily due to investments in funds with lower fees. Fund fees are also lower for investors who hold a retirement fund; and post-FOFA. In both cases, lower fund fees translate into better risk-adjusted returns. Total service fees, negotiated in dollar terms and not as fixed percentages, average 0.56% of assets annually for self-directed investors and 0.82% for advised investors.18 This results in an average advisory fee of 0.26% per year.Table 5. Investor characteristics and portfolio qualityThe evidence in Table 6 on the control variables discussed in Section 2.3 indicates that log Assets, log Age, and 1Retire proxy for investment sophistication. In particular, wealthier investors have significantly higher net abnormal returns (coefficient 0.008), including net of total service fees (coefficient 0.011). This is due entirely to lower fund fees (coefficient ‚ąí0.010). Wealthier investors do not earn higher gross abnormal returns, despite holding funds with more idiosyncratic risk. They maintain higher equity allocations (1.742) and are more diversified across asset classes (0.236), but they have greater home bias. These results are generally consistent with Calvet et al. (2007).Log Age is also positively related to net abnormal returns (coefficient 0.020), due to both lower fund fees and higher gross fund returns. Age predicts less home bias and less equity exposure, consistent with Kramer (2012) and Madrian and Shea (2001). The indicator 1Retire identifies lower fund fees and active share, better diversification and lower home bias, but not significantly higher abnormal returns. While the inertia proxies log DaysInv and log DaysBank are both associated with lower net abnormal returns, they yield opposing inferences in several columns of Table 6. Since they are similar in magnitude, we focus on their sum. Consistent with Madrian and Shea (2001), we find that inertia lessens portfolio quality (lower returns, higher fees, less diversification, and higher home bias). Likewise, consistent with the overconfidence literature (Barber and Odean, 2001, Barber et al., 2005, Barber et al., 2009), males hold funds with lower abnormal returns, greater active share, higher fees, less diversification, and greater equity exposure and home bias. 1Employed generally aligns with 1Male, suggesting both proxy for overconfidence.4.4. Baseline inferences on value from adviceThe coefficient on 1Adv in Table 6 indicates that fee-based advice yields a statistically reliable 4.4 bps per month (53 bps annual) increase in net abnormal returns. This result derives from both lower fund fees (1.8 bps per month) and higher gross abnormal returns (2.5 bps monthly, marginally significant). A relevant question is whether this marginal return associated with advice covers its marginal cost. A novel feature of fee-based advice (and our data) is that we can directly assess this question using the differential service fee for advice. From Table 6, the net abnormal return of advised investors after service fees is higher (coefficient 0.19), but the relation is not statistically reliable. Thus, investors reliably cover costs, but a net benefit is less clear. We get sharper inferences on advice benefits later (Sections 5.1 Investor response to FOFA nudges conditioned on instrumented value of advice, 5.2 Differential advice benefits conditional on dropping in response to a FOFA nudge), conditioning on FOFA nudges as an investor-selection mechanism.Our conclusion on returns aligns with Bhattacharya et al. (2012), who observe that investor portfolio performance would have improved had they followed the advice provided to them. However, our evidence contrasts with studies based on stock or mutual fund trading records under commission-based advice, which typically find that advice yields lower returns (Hackethal et al., 2012, Hoechle et al., 2017, Chalmers and Reuter, 2020). Further support for an informational role of fee-based advice is seen with the positive relation (0.468) to the active share (idiosyncratic risk) in funds held. Kramer (2012) finds lower idiosyncratic risk in advised portfolios, while Bhattacharya et al. (2012) find no significant difference.Fee-based advice also improves portfolio quality on other dimensions. There is a 76 bps higher allocation to equity with advice. As with active share, the literature reports mixed results here. Kramer (2012) finds lower equity allocations with advice, whereas Bhattacharya et al. (2012), Foerster et al. (2017), and Chalmers and Reuter (2020) report higher equity allocations.19 Diversification across asset classes improves with advice (0.098), consistent with the literature. However, home bias also increases, contrary to Kramer (2012) and Hoechle et al. (2017). Interestingly, Bhattacharya et al. (2012) find that even when advice would reduce home bias, investors often fail to follow that advice.4.5. In addition to its descriptive contribution, this analysis yields an instrument for heterogeneity that we later use to assess the precision of FOFA in nudging out investors who do not benefit from advice (Table 8).An appropriate prescription for risk exposure (e.g., Equity, Diversification) arguably increases with investment horizon. From Table 7, the (‚ąí0.996) coefficient on the interactive 1Adv‚čÖ log Age in the Equity regression supports this prescription. Likewise, 1Adv‚čÖ1Retire is associated with reliably higher equity allocations (4.3), consistent with a longer investment horizon for these accounts. Finally, the coefficients in the Diversification and Active regressions also imply lower risk with advice for older investors. As in Table 4, the dependent variable is an indicator for having dropped advice as of the observation month (0‚Äď11 following the event listed in the column heading). ‚ÄėBaseline‚Äô means no FOFA event; FDS means receipt of first fee disclosure statement; and Opt-in means receipt of second FDS and first Opt-in notice. Statistical significance (1, 2, and 3 bars at 10%, 5%, and 1%) is computed with robust standard errors.Of the three sophistication proxies considered in Table 7 (assets, age, and retirement), only log Age has a moderately reliable (10% significance) interactive coefficient for abnormal returns (‚ąí0.013). However, the relation is reliably negative for abnormal returns net of service fees (‚ąí0.016). We conclude that younger investors benefit more from advice. Recommendations are less clouded by noisy returns in the case of fund fees. Here, advice reliably benefits unsophisticated investors. Fund fees are lower for both young and less wealthy investors. Overall, we infer higher advice benefits for less sophisticated investors.Regarding inertia, we collectively consider the coefficients on log DaysInv and log DaysBk. Advice yields more benefit for higher inertia investors via: lower fund fees (coefficients ‚ąí0.013 and 0.002); higher AbnRets (coefficients moderately reliable at 0.011 and 0.018); higher AbnRet‚ÄďServFees (coefficients 0.14 and 0.17); and reduced equity exposure (‚ąí1.058 and ‚ąí0.720). Regarding overconfidence, interacting 1Adv with 1Male and 1Employed identifies lower fund fees (‚ąí0.001 and ‚ąí0.004); higher AbnRets (0.003 and 0.018); higher AbnRet‚ÄďServFees (0.003 and 0.016); and reduced home bias (‚ąí0.250 and ‚ąí3.809).Collectively, we conclude that fee-based advice provides more benefit to relatively unsophisticated; overconfident; and inattentive investors.
article.5. Are FOFA nudges beneficial for invest: The preceding analyses establish that both the FDS and opt-in nudges trigger drops of advice (Sections Section 3.3, 3.4), and that the response varies by investor characteristics 3.5. Moreover, advice can be beneficial 4.4, and the degree of benefit varies by investor characteristics 4.5. We now evaluate whether FOFA nudges induce more advice drops for investors receiving fewer benefits from advice‚ÄĒboth before and after considering advice fees.5.1. Investor response to FOFA nudges conditioned on instrumented value of adviceThe analysis in Table 8 parallels that of Table 4, using a linear probability model to examine the impact of FOFA nudges on advice drop rates. The difference here is that we condition on a composite instrument for advice value (denoted AdviceValue) rather than individual investor characteristics with no consideration of advice benefits. We also include past-return interactives as controls. Gross AdviceValue is the dot product A‚čÖB where A is the vector of estimated coefficients on 1Adv‚čÖChars(j) {j= log Assets through 1Retire} in the AbnRet regression of Table 7; and B is the corresponding vector of investor characteristics as of event time (Base, FDS, or Opt-in anniversary).20 Net AdviceValue uses the AbnRet‚ÄďServFee regression coefficients from Table 7.5.1.1. Do FDS nudges discriminate on advice value?From Panel A of Table 8, the coefficient on gross AdviceValue is negative for baseline drops (‚ąí0.002, reliable at 1%), thus investors with less AdviceValue more likely drop advice independent of FOFA nudges. From Panel B, this relation is even stronger with net AdviceValue. We conclude that a subset of investors are naturally attentive to a cost/benefit assessment of advice, and that attention improves their advice decisions.The FDS nudge nearly triples the effectiveness of investors‚Äô baseline cost-benefit assessment of advice. From Panel A of Table 8, the interactive coefficient on gross AdviceValue is ‚ąí0.009, as is the coefficient on net AdviceValue in Panel B. Thus, investors who benefit more from advice are significantly less likely to drop following an FDS, both gross or net of advice fees. This incremental effect of the FDS is about twice baseline values (‚ąí0.002 for gross AdviceValue and ‚ąí0.005 for net AdviceValue).5.1.2. Does the opt-in nudge properly discriminate advice value?The evidence in Table 8 on auto-drop is not favorable. In Panel A, the coefficient on gross AdviceValue, when interacted with the indicator for an opt-in event, is a statistically reliable 0.090. This implies more drops in the 12 months following an opt-in notice for investors who receive greater benefits from advice. This outcome is contrary to the presumed regulatory objective of selectively terminating advice for investors receiving less benefit. In Panel B, the coefficient on net AdviceValue is much smaller, but still significantly positive, at 0.007. The difference arises because investors dropping after the opt-in notice (mostly auto-drops) tend to pay high percentage advice fees, averaging about 92% of gross AdviceValue.An important observation here is the apparent role of fixed costs in providing advice. Assuming the service provider sets (dollar) advice fees to (partially) recover fixed costs, the percentage advice fee can be quite high for less wealthy investors (who tend to be auto-dropped). Thus, even though less wealthy investors benefit more from advice gross of fees (see Section 4.5), advice is less cost‚Äďeffective for them with dollar-based pricing.5.2. Differential advice benefits conditional on dropping in response to a FOFA nudgeIn the preceding analysis we evaluate how FOFA nudges differentially affect investors‚Äô drop decision using an ex ante instrument (i.e., expected advice benefits conditioning on investor characteristics). In Table 9 we provide a robustness analysis, flipping the perspective and evaluating advice benefits conditional on the ex post (actual) circumstances of an investor‚Äôs drop. That is, we assess differential benefits interacting the advice indicator (1Adv) with three indicators for the circumstances of a drop. 1AutoDT (DT = drop type) indicates an investor who drops advice either two or three months after an opt-in notice, indicating auto-drop. 1FDSDT is a drop within six months of an FDS where 1AutoDT=0. This indicates a response to the FDS uncontaminated by auto-drop. 1BaseDT is all other drops. The default case (where all indicators are zero) is an investor who does not drop. Indicator values are constant for each investor, so they indicate an investor type. An important distinction of Table 9 (relative to 8) is that it quantifies the value of advice across types.The coefficient on 1Adv in each of the four regressions in Table 9 indicates that investors who retain advice receive relatively high abnormal returns, both gross (0.085) and net (0.063) of service fees. This performance improvement is about equally attributable to lower fund fees (‚ąí0.040) and higher gross fund returns (0.045). Investors who drop advice without an FDS notice (i.e., Base) benefit less from advice, as evidenced by their lower abnormal return before fees (‚ąí0.059) and net of service fees (‚ąí0.066). Investors who drop advice following an FDS notice benefit even less from advice, as evidenced by their lower abnormal return before fees (‚ąí0.073) and net of service fees (‚ąí0.080). Investors who are auto-dropped do not have a statistically reliably difference in benefit relative to those who retain advice. Auto-dropped investors‚Äô advice benefits are positive, as indicated by the significant unconditional coefficient for net-of-service-fee returns (0.063) plus the insignificant conditional (‚ąí0.019).Table 9. However, involuntary auto-drops do not discriminate investors based on benefit from advice.5.3. Persistent benefits from advice even after dropsThe preceding evidence suggests that fee-based advice generally improves portfolio quality, and that investors generally make good decisions regarding continued payment for advice, particularly in response to the FDS. A natural question is whether advice benefits persist even after advice is dropped. That is, do investors learn from and maintain the benefits of advice? We consider this for three categories of drops ‚ÄĒ those in response to an FDS, auto-drops, and other (e.g., pre FDS).The first row of Table 10 begins with a tabulation of the magnitude of portfolio changes following a drop of advice. Generally, dropping investors maintain substantially the same portfolio. The mean (across investors) total change in portfolio holdings over the twelve months following a drop is 3.8% of the portfolio.21 The table then reports on the change in portfolio characteristics arising from these changes in composition. More specifically, we compute (for example) the abnormal returns (gross of service fees) of the hypothetical extrapolated portfolio each month post-drop, and the actual return in the corresponding month. We average this difference across the twelve months following the drop, and then average across investors within each category of drop.Given the limited change in portfolio, the delta for abnormal returns relative to status quo is minor and generally insignificant. However, the delta for fund fees is statistically reliably positive in the case of auto-drops and drops without a nudge. In the case of FDS drops, fund fees decline by a statistically irrelevant amount. Diversification improves, but home bias worsens, similarly for both FDS and auto-drops. The caveat of limited time-series data is that there is no reliable evidence of a reversal of advice benefits in the 12 months following auto-drop. This is consistent with the persistence of benefits even after being dropped. This inference is all the more plausible in the case of FDS-induced drops.
article.6. Conclusion: Australia‚Äôs natural experiment in the regulation of financial advice ‚Äď FOFA ‚Äď yields novel and important insights on the role of salience in fee disclosure and on the impact of an unusually proactive auto-drop for regulating the continuation of advice services. We find strong evidence that salience is an important feature of disclosure. However, we find even stronger evidence of substantial residual inattention, irrespective of salient disclosure. In particular, auto-drop ‚Äď the regulatory response to a failure to opt-in to advice continuance ‚ÄĒ occurs at seven times the normal drop rate for advice. Auto-drop provides a direct measure of inattention to concurrent disclosure.We analyze the value of fee-based advice and find that it benefits investors on several dimensions. Benefits are stronger for relatively unsophisticated investors and those susceptible to inertia and overconfidence. Investors‚Äô decisions regarding advice are somewhat successful in discriminating advice benefits, but FOFA‚Äôs disclosure requirement strongly improves discrimination of advice benefits. However, auto-drop does not successfully discriminate investors, as auto-dropped investors were receiving above-average value from advice.This leads to our central conclusion. To be effective, regulation of costly financial services must engage the investor. Brute force (e.g., auto-drop) has high impact but ‚Äď at least in this case ‚Äď it is ineffective. Furthermore, we find that even highly salient fee disclosure fails to engage many investors. This underscores the primary challenge for regulators: finding ways to foster the investor engagement needed to enhance decision-making in financial advice.
